name: roktools

metadata.name: roktools
metadata.summary: Package with utilities and tools for GNSS data processing

documentation:
# pyrok-tools

Python tools used in internal Rokubun projects. This repository contains the following modules:

- `logger`, a module that extends basic Python logging
- `geodetic`, to perform basic geodetic transformation (Cartesian to Geodetic,
  Cartesian to Local Tangential Plane, ...)

## Installation

To make sure that the extensions are installed along with the package, run

`pip install roktools*.whl`


## Modules

### Logger

Example of how to use the logger module:
```python
>>> from roktools import logger
>>> logger.set_level("DEBUG")
>>> logger.debug("Debug message")
2020-05-05 18:23:55,688 - DEBUG    - Debug message
>>> logger.warning("Warning message")
2020-05-05 18:24:11,327 - WARNING  - Warning message
>>> logger.info("Info message")
2020-05-05 18:24:26,021 - INFO     - Info message
>>> logger.error("Error message")
2020-05-05 18:24:36,090 - ERROR    - Error message
>>> logger.critical("Critical message")
2020-05-05 18:24:43,562 - CRITICAL - Critical message
>>> logger.exception("Exception message", ValueError("Exception message")
2020-05-05 18:25:11,360 - CRITICAL - Exception message
ValueError: Exception message
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/Users/alexlopez/Work/00.General/01.Software/py-roktools/roktools/logger.py", line 46, in exception
    raise exception
ValueError: Exception message
```


## Deployment to PyPi

The project is published automatically using internal Gitlab CI on each commit to master to PyPi repository [roktools](https://pypi.org/project/roktools/)

It uses semantic versioning and conventional commits to set the version and [semantic-release](https://python-semantic-release.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html) as
versioning tool.

modules.name: setup
documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: roktools.parsers.rtklib.stats.parse
documentation:
Loads $SAT lines of a.stat file from a rtklib solution
The format of those files are the following:
Residuals of pseudorange and carrier-phase observables. The format
of a record is as follows.
$SAT,week,tow,sat,frq,az,el,resp,resc,vsat,snr,fix,slip,lock,outc,slipc,rejc,icbias,bias,bias_var,lambda
   - week/tow : gps week no/time of week (s)
   - sat/frq : satellite id/frequency (1:L1,2:L2,3:L5,...)
   - az/el : azimuth/elevation angle (deg)
   - resp : pseudorange residual (m)
   - resc : carrier-phase residual (m)
   - vsat : valid data flag (0:invalid,1:valid)
   - snr : signal strength (dbHz)
   - fix : ambiguity flag (0:no data,1:not part of AR set,2:part of AR set,3:part of hold set)
   - slip : cycle-slip flag (bit1:slip,bit2:parity unknown)
   - lock : carrier-lock count
   - outc : data outage count
   - slipc : cycle-slip count
   - rejc : data reject (outlier) count
   - icbias : interchannel bias (GLONASS)
   - bias : phase bias
   - bias_var : variance of phase bias
   - lambda : wavelength


Example:
$SAT,2215,152783.000,G03,1,82.8,35.9,16.3975,0.0018,1,36,0,0,0,0,0,0,-117643836.98,182.318392,0.00000

Args:
    stat_file (str): .stat file path

Returns:
    np.array: A numpy array with the content of the file

functions.name: roktools.parsers.rtklib.stats.__add_field_in_numpy_array
documentation:
Return a new array that is like "a", but has additional fields.

Arguments:

: a -- a structured numpy array
  descr -- a numpy type description of the new fields

The contents of "a" are copied over to the appropriate fields in
the new array, whereas the new fields are uninitialized. The
arguments are not modified.

```pycon
>>> sa = numpy.array([(1, 'Foo'), (2, 'Bar')], dtype=[('id', '<i8'), ('name', '|S3')])
>>> sa.dtype.descr
[('id', '<i8'), ('name', '|S3')]
```

```pycon
>>> sb = __add_field_in_numpy_array(sa, [('score', '<f8')])
>>> sb.dtype.descr
[('id', '<i8'), ('name', '|S3'), ('score', '<f8')]
```

```pycon
>>> numpy.all(sa['id'] == sb['id'])
True
>>> numpy.all(sa['name'] == sb['name'])
True
```

functions.name: roktools.file.grep_lines
documentation:
Generator function used to grep lines from a file. Can be used in methods
such as numpy.genfromtxt, ...

```pycon
>>> generator = grep_lines(filename, "pattern")
>>> data = numpy.loadtxt(generator)
```

functions.name: roktools.time.to_week_tow
documentation:
Convert from datetime to GPS week (asumes datetime in GPS Timescale)

```pycon
>>> to_week_tow(datetime.datetime(1980, 1, 6))
WeekTow(week=0, tow=0.0, day_of_week=0)
>>> to_week_tow(datetime.datetime(2005, 1, 28, 13, 30))
WeekTow(week=1307, tow=480600.0, day_of_week=5)
```

Conversion method based on algorithm provided in this link
<http://www.novatel.com/support/knowledge-and-learning/published-papers-and-documents/unit-conversions/>

functions.name: roktools.time.from_week_tow
documentation:
Convert from week tow to datetime in GPS scale

```pycon
>>> from_week_tow(0, 0.0)
datetime.datetime(1980, 1, 6, 0, 0)
>>> from_week_tow(1307, 480600.0)
datetime.datetime(2005, 1, 28, 13, 30)
```

functions.name: roktools.time.weektow_to_j2000
documentation:
Convert from GPS week and time of the week (in seconds) to j2000 seconds

The week and tow values can be vectors, and thus it will return a vector of
tuples.

```pycon
>>> weektow_to_j2000(0, 0.0)
-630763200.0
```

functions.name: roktools.time.to_j2000
documentation:
Convert from datetime toj2000 seconds

```pycon
>>> to_j2000(datetime.datetime(2005, 1, 28, 13, 30))
160191000.0
```

functions.name: roktools.time.from_j2000
documentation:
Convert from J2000 epoch to datetime

```pycon
>>> from_j2000(160191000)
datetime.datetime(2005, 1, 28, 13, 30)
```

```pycon
>>> from_j2000(160191000, fraction_of_seconds = 0.1)
datetime.datetime(2005, 1, 28, 13, 30, 0, 100000)
```

functions.name: roktools.time.epoch_range
documentation:
Iterate between 2 epochs with a given interval

```pycon
>>> import datetime
>>> st = datetime.datetime(2015, 10, 1,  0,  0,  0)
>>> en = datetime.datetime(2015, 10, 1,  0, 59, 59)
>>> interval_s = 15 * 60
>>> ','.join([str(d) for d in epoch_range(st, en, interval_s)])
'2015-10-01 00:00:00,2015-10-01 00:15:00,2015-10-01 00:30:00,2015-10-01 00:45:00'
>>> st = datetime.datetime(2015, 10, 1,  0,  0,  0)
>>> en = datetime.datetime(2015, 10, 1,  1,  0,  0)
>>> interval_s = 15 * 60
>>> ','.join([str(d) for d in epoch_range(st, en, interval_s)])
'2015-10-01 00:00:00,2015-10-01 00:15:00,2015-10-01 00:30:00,2015-10-01 00:45:00,2015-10-01 01:00:00'
```

functions.name: roktools.time.round_to_interval
documentation:
```pycon
>>> dt = datetime.datetime(2023, 4, 20, 10, 48, 52, 794000)
>>> interval = 0.1
>>> round_to_interval(dt, interval)
datetime.datetime(2023, 4, 20, 10, 48, 52, 800000)
```

```pycon
>>> interval = 1.0
>>> round_to_interval(dt, interval)
datetime.datetime(2023, 4, 20, 10, 48, 53)
```

```pycon
>>> interval = 0.5
>>> round_to_interval(dt, interval)
datetime.datetime(2023, 4, 20, 10, 48, 53)
```

```pycon
>>> interval = 2.0
>>> round_to_interval(dt, interval)
datetime.datetime(2023, 4, 20, 10, 48, 52)
```

```pycon
>>> interval = 0.05
>>> round_to_interval(dt, interval)
datetime.datetime(2023, 4, 20, 10, 48, 52, 800000)
```

```pycon
>>> interval = 0.01
>>> round_to_interval(dt, interval)
datetime.datetime(2023, 4, 20, 10, 48, 52, 790000)
```

functions.name: roktools.time.get_interval
documentation:
Finds the closest possible interval from a predefined set of intervals to the computed inteval
from the pvt.

Args:

: epochs: List of datetimes
  interval: The target intervals for which the closest possible interval is to be found.

Returns:

: The closest possible interval from the predefined set.

```pycon
>>> t0 = datetime.datetime(2023, 6, 1, 12, 0, 0)
>>> epochs = [t0, datetime.datetime(2023, 6, 1, 12, 0, 2), datetime.datetime(2023, 6, 1, 12, 0, 3)]
>>> get_interval(epochs)
2.0
```

```pycon
>>> t2 = datetime.datetime(2023, 6, 1, 12, 0, 1)
>>> epochs = [t0, t2, datetime.datetime(2023, 6, 1, 12, 0, 2)]
>>> get_interval(epochs)
1.0
```

```pycon
>>> t1 = datetime.datetime(2023, 6, 1, 12, 0, 0, 500000)
>>> epochs = [t0, t1, t2]
>>> get_interval(epochs)
0.5
```

```pycon
>>> t1 = datetime.datetime(2023, 6, 1, 12, 0, 0, 100000)
>>> t2 = datetime.datetime(2023, 6, 1, 12, 0, 0, 200000)
>>> epochs = [t0, t1, t2]
>>> get_interval(epochs)
0.1
```

```pycon
>>> t1 = datetime.datetime(2023, 6, 1, 12, 0, 0, 10000)
>>> t2 = datetime.datetime(2023, 6, 1, 12, 0, 0, 11000)
>>> epochs = [t0, t1, t2]
>>> get_interval(epochs)
0.01
```

functions.name: roktools.time.to_julian_date
documentation:
Convert an epoch to Julian Date

```pycon
>>> to_julian_date(datetime.datetime(2024, 2, 11))
2460351.5
>>> round(to_julian_date(datetime.datetime(2019, 1, 1, 8)), 2)
2458484.83
```

functions.name: roktools.time.seconds_of_day
documentation:
Compute the seconds of the day

```pycon
>>> seconds_of_day(datetime.datetime(2024, 4, 1))
0.0
>>> seconds_of_day(datetime.datetime(2024, 4, 1, 23, 59, 59))
86399.0
```

functions.name: roktools.time.gmst
documentation:
Compute the Greenwich Mean Sidereal Time (in hours)

<https://astronomy.stackexchange.com/questions/21002/how-to-find-greenwich-mean-sideral-time>

```pycon
>>> round(gmst(datetime.datetime(2019, 1, 1, 8)), 6)
14.712605
>>> gmst_hour = gmst(datetime.datetime(2024, 2, 11))
>>> round(gmst_hour * math.tau / 24, 9)
2.453307616
```

functions.name: roktools.stats.rms
documentation:
Compute the Root Mean Square of an array of values

```pycon
>>> array = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
>>> rms(array)
np.float64(3.3166247903554)
```

modules.name: roktools.tensorial
documentation:
This script is used to compare two files so that they are put side by
side.

Usage:
      cat file1 file2 | tensorial.py -c col1 [col2 [col3]] ...



Examples:
  (a) Join two files using the label in column 1 as a reference
        cat f1.txt f2.txt | tensorial.py -c 1

functions.name: roktools.orbit.kepler.compute_semi_major_axis
documentation:
Compute the semi major axis (a) in meters from the mean motion

Using the equation \$\$n = sqrt(mu) / (sqrt(A))^3\$\$

```pycon
>>> mean_motion_rev_per_day = 15.5918272
>>> mean_motion_rad_per_s = mean_motion_rev_per_day * math.tau / 86400.0
>>> compute_semi_major_axis(mean_motion_rad_per_s)
6768158.4970976645
```

methods.name: roktools.gnss.types.ConstellationId.from_string
documentation:
Returns the corresponding ConstellationId from input character

```pycon
>>> c = ConstellationId.GPS
>>> c.to_char()
'G'
```

methods.name: roktools.gnss.types.ConstellationId.__lt__
documentation:
Less operator for the ConstellationId class

```pycon
>>> c1 = ConstellationId.GPS
>>> c2 = ConstellationId.IRNSS
>>> c1 < c2
True
>>> c2 < c1
False
>>> c2 < 0
Traceback (most recent call last):
...
TypeError: '<' not supported between instances of 'ConstellationId' and 'int'
```

documentation: Init the class (does nothing)

methods.name: roktools.gnss.types.Band.get_freq_from_rinex_band
documentation:
Get the band from the RINEX band number (and constellation)

```pycon
>>> Band.get_freq_from_rinex_band(ConstellationId.GPS, 1)
1575420000
>>> Band.get_freq_from_rinex_band('G', 1)
1575420000
>>> Band.get_freq_from_rinex_band(ConstellationId.GALILEO, 5)
1176450000
>>> Band.get_freq_from_rinex_band('E', 5)
1176450000
>>> Band.get_freq_from_rinex_band(ConstellationId.GPS, 8)
Traceback (most recent call last):
...
ValueError: Invalid band 8 for constellation ConstellationId.GPS
```
Namespace to define the supported bands (center frequency in Hz)

methods.name: roktools.gnss.types.TrackingChannel.from_observable_type
documentation:
Deprecated: This function is deprecated and will be removed in the future.
Use the from_rinex3_code() method instead.

Extract the tracking channel from a RINEX3 observable type

methods.name: roktools.gnss.types.TrackingChannel.from_rinex3_code
documentation:
Extract the tracking channel from a RINEX3 observable type

```pycon
>>> TrackingChannel.from_rinex3_code('C1C')
1C
```

methods.name: roktools.gnss.types.TrackingChannel.from_string
documentation:
Extract the tracking channel from a string that indicates the tracking
channel based on the last two characters of the corresponding RINEX3
code

```pycon
>>> TrackingChannel.from_string('1C')
1C
```

methods.name: roktools.gnss.types.TrackingChannel.get_frequency
documentation:
Get the frequency (in Hz) from the tracking channel band

```pycon
>>> ch = TrackingChannel(1, 'C')
>>> ch.get_frequency(ConstellationId.GPS)
1575420000
>>> ch.get_frequency('G')
1575420000
```

methods.name: roktools.gnss.types.TrackingChannel.get_wavelength
documentation:
Get the wavelength (in meters) from the tracking channel band

```pycon
>>> ch = TrackingChannel(1, 'C')
>>> wl_m = ch.get_wavelength(ConstellationId.GPS)
>>> int(wl_m * 100)
19
>>> wl_m = ch.get_wavelength('G')
>>> int(wl_m * 100)
19
```

functions.name: roktools.rinex.strftime
documentation:
```pycon
>>> epoch = datetime.datetime(2019, 8, 3, 10, 10, 10)
>>> strftime(epoch, "ebre215${rinexhour}${rinexquarter}.19o")
'ebre215k00.19o'
```

```pycon
>>> epoch = datetime.datetime(2019, 8, 3, 10, 50, 10)
>>> strftime(epoch, "ebre215${RINEXHOUR}${rinexQUARTER}.19o")
'ebre215k45.19o'
```

```pycon
>>> epoch = datetime.datetime(2019, 8, 3, 0, 0, 0)
>>> strftime(epoch, "ebre215${rinexhour}${rinexquarter}.19o")
'ebre215a00.19o'
```

```pycon
>>> epoch = datetime.datetime(2019, 8, 3, 23, 50, 10)
>>> strftime(epoch, "ebre215${rinexhour}${rinexquarter}.19o")
'ebre215x45.19o'
```

functions.name: roktools.rinex.get_quarter_str
documentation:
Get the Rinex quarter string ("00", "15", "30", "45") for a given number of seconds

```pycon
>>> get_quarter_str(100)
0
>>> get_quarter_str(920)
15
>>> get_quarter_str(1800)
30
>>> get_quarter_str(2900)
45
>>> get_quarter_str(3600 + 900)
15
```

functions.name: roktools.rinex.get_channels
documentation:
Get the channel list from a list of observables

```pycon
>>> C1C = ObservableType.from_string("C1C")
>>> L1C = ObservableType.from_string("L1C")
>>> C1W = ObservableType.from_string("C1W")
>>> C2W = ObservableType.from_string("C2W")
>>> L2W = ObservableType.from_string("L2W")
>>> C2L = ObservableType.from_string("C2L")
>>> L2L = ObservableType.from_string("L2L")
>>> C5Q = ObservableType.from_string("C5Q")
>>> L5Q = ObservableType.from_string("L5Q")
>>> get_channels([C1C, L1C, C1W, C2W, L2W, C2L, L2L, C5Q, L5Q])
[1C, 1W, 2W, 2L, 5Q]
```

functions.name: roktools.rinex.get_obs_mapping
documentation:
Get the observable mappings for a constellation

```pycon
>>> line = "G    9 C1C L1C C1W C2W L2W C2L L2L C5Q L5Q                  SYS / # / OBS TYPES"
>>> get_obs_mapping([line])
{'G': [C1C, L1C, C1W, C2W, L2W, C2L, L2L, C5Q, L5Q]}
```

```pycon
>>> lines = ["G   20 C1C L1C D1C S1C C1W L1W D1W S1W C2W L2W D2W S2W C2L  SYS / # / OBS TYPES",                  "       L2L D2L S2L C5Q L5Q D5Q S5Q                          SYS / # / OBS TYPES"]
>>> get_obs_mapping(lines)
{'G': [C1C, L1C, D1C, S1C, C1W, L1W, D1W, S1W, C2W, L2W, D2W, S2W, C2L, L2L, D2L, S2L, C5Q, L5Q, D5Q, S5Q]}
```

functions.name: roktools.rinex._parse_obs_line
documentation:
```pycon
>>> line = "C05  40058862.469 6 208597044.05206  40058858.572 7 161300483.44407  40058861.947 7 169502210.29507"
>>> _parse_obs_line(line, 6)
(C05, [ObservableValue(value=40058862.469, lli=0, snr=6), ObservableValue(value=208597044.052, lli=0, snr=6), ObservableValue(value=40058858.572, lli=0, snr=7), ObservableValue(value=161300483.444, lli=0, snr=7), ObservableValue(value=40058861.947, lli=0, snr=7), ObservableValue(value=169502210.295, lli=0, snr=7)])
```

functions.name: roktools.rinex._parse_rnx3_epoch
documentation:
Parse a measurement epoch from a Rinex3 and return a tuple
with the epochm event type and number of lines

```pycon
>>> _parse_rnx3_epoch("> 2017 08 03 11 22 30.1234000  0 29")
(datetime.datetime(2017, 8, 3, 11, 22, 30, 123400), 0, 29)
```

```pycon
>>> _parse_rnx3_epoch("> 2021  2  5 15 51 30.2000000 0 22")
(datetime.datetime(2021, 2, 5, 15, 51, 30, 200000), 0, 22)
```

```pycon
>>> _parse_rnx3_epoch("> 2020 11 18 21 43 30.0000000  0 28       0.000000000000")
(datetime.datetime(2020, 11, 18, 21, 43, 30), 0, 28)
```

```pycon
>>> _parse_rnx3_epoch("> 2019 07 02 13 25  5.9999995  0 31")
(datetime.datetime(2019, 7, 2, 13, 25, 5, 999999), 0, 31)
```

functions.name: roktools.rinex._get_eph_type_from_rinex4_nav_block_header
documentation:
Get the type of ephemeris based on the header line of the Rinex 4 navigation block

```pycon
>>> _get_eph_type_from_rinex4_nav_block_header('> EPH G01 LNAV')
<EphType.GPS_LNAV: 'G_LNAV'>
```

```pycon
>>> _get_eph_type_from_rinex4_nav_block_header('> EPH X02434')
<EphType.STARLINK: 'X'>
```

methods.name: roktools.rinex.EphType.from_string

Get the Ephemerides type from an input string (or raise an exception if not found)

```pycon

<EphType.GPS_LNAV: 'G_LNAV'>

methods.name: roktools.rinex.GnssRandomClock.__init__
documentation:
Initialize the object with the threshold values for the bias and drift.
In certain works, the typical values of bias and drift for GPS, Galileo
and Beidou are usually contained between -/+0.5ms and-/+1e-11 respectively

Generate a clock model with random realizations for the clock bias and
drift.

ValueError: The line [ None ] does not have an Epoch Flag
>>> EpochFlag.get_from_line("")
Traceback (most recent call last):
...
ValueError: The line [  ] does not have an Epoch Flag

Traceback (most recent call last):
...
ValueError: The line [ > 2023 08 03 1 ] does not have an Epoch Flag
```

functions.name: roktools.orbit.tle.parse_decimal_point
documentation:
Parse float numers with decimal point assumed and exponent

```pycon
>>> parse_decimal_point("0006703")
0.0006703
>>> f'{parse_decimal_point("-11606-4"):.4e}'
'-1.1606e-05'
>>> f'{parse_decimal_point("-11606+4"):.4e}'
'-1.1606E+03'
>>> f'{parse_decimal_point(" 11606-4"):.4e}'
'1.1606e-05'
```

modules.name: roktools.cl
documentation:
Program to perform various columnar operations on inputs

All indicators have this format

 'x0'

where 'x' can be one of the following

- 'c' - Select column
- 'd' - diff column relative to the previous value
- 'f' - diff column relative to the first value of the column
- 'm' - Compute the minutes elapsed since the first value (divide column by 60,
        as it assumes that the values are in seconds))
- 'h' - Compute the hours elapsed since the first value (divide column by 3600,
        as it assumes that the values are in seconds))

and '0' is the column number (1 based)

Examples:

(a) Select columns with the indicated order (first output 5th column and then the
    first column)
        cat file.txt | cl c5 c1

(b) Select 6th column and output 1st column relative to the first one
        cat file.txt | cl c6 f1

(c) Make a diff of the third column relative to the first value
        cat file.txt | cl f3

documentation: Module with several geodetic related methods

functions.name: roktools.geodetic.convert_decimal_units_to_dms
documentation:
Convert from longitude/latitude to Degrees, minutes and seconds
\>>> latitude_deg = 41.528442316
\>>> dms = convert_decimal_units_to_dms(latitude_deg)
\>>> "{:.0f} {:.0f} {:.7f}".format(dms)
'41 31 42.3923376'
\>>> longitude_deg = 2.434318900
\>>> dms = convert_decimal_units_to_dms(longitude_deg)
\>>> "{:.0f} {:.0f} {:.7f}".format(dms)
'2 26 3.5480400'

functions.name: roktools.geodetic.xyz_to_lla
documentation:
Convert from Cartesian XYZ-ECEF coordinates to geodetic coordinates
(relative to a reference ellipsoid which defaults to WGS84).

Output longitude and latitude are expressed in degrees

The x, y and z values can be vectors

```pycon
>>> x, y, z = xyz_to_lla(4807314.3520, 98057.0330, 4176767.6160)
>>> assert math.isclose(x, 1.1685266980613551)
>>> assert math.isclose(y, 41.170001652314625)
>>> assert math.isclose(z, 173.4421242615208)
```

```pycon
>>> xs = np.array([4807314.3520, 4807315.3520, 4807316.3520])
>>> ys = np.array([98057.0330, 98058.0330, 98059.0330])
>>> zs = np.array([4176767.6160, 4176768.6160, 4176769.6160])
>>> lon, lat, height = xyz_to_lla(xs, ys, zs)
>>> "{0:.7f}".format(lon[0])
'1.1685267'
>>> "{0:.7f}".format(lon[1])
'1.1685384'
>>> "{0:.7f}".format(lon[2])
'1.1685500'
>>> "{0:.7f}".format(lat[0])
'41.1700017'
>>> "{0:.7f}".format(lat[1])
'41.1700024'
>>> "{0:.7f}".format(lat[2])
'41.1700031'
>>> "{0:.7f}".format(height[0])
'173.4421243'
>>> "{0:.7f}".format(height[1])
'174.8683741'
>>> "{0:.7f}".format(height[2])
'176.2946241'
```

functions.name: roktools.geodetic.body_to_enu_matrix
documentation:
Computes the matrix that transforms from Body frame to ENU

This accepts scalars or vector of scalars.

Also, the yaw, pitch and roll can be expressed as radians (radians=True)
or degrees (radians=False)

functions.name: roktools.geodetic.body_to_enu
documentation:
Converts from Body reference frame to ENU reference point.

This accepts scalars or vector of scalars.

Also, the yaw, pitch and roll can be expressed as radians (radians=True)
or degrees (radians=False)

TEST RATIONALE HAS TO BE CHANGED ACCORDING TO THE CHANGE IN THE BODY_TO_ENU_MATRIX RESULT INTERPRETATION:
BODY_TO_ENU_MATRIX IS ACTUALLY IMPLEMENTING BODY2NED_MATRIX!!!!!!

```pycon
>>> body_to_enu(0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 1)
(np.float64(1.0), np.float64(1.0), np.float64(-1.0))
```

```pycon
>>> enu = body_to_enu(90, 0, 0, 1, 1, 1)
>>> np.round(enu)
array([ 1., -1., -1.])
```

```pycon
>>> enu = body_to_enu(0, 90, 0, 1, 1, 1)
>>> np.round(enu)
array([1., 1., 1.])
```

```pycon
>>> enu = body_to_enu(0, 0, 90, 1, 1, 1)
>>> np.round(enu)
array([-1.,  1., -1.])
```

```pycon
>>> yaws = [0] * 3; pitches = [0] * 3; rolls = [0] * 3
>>> xs = [1] * 3; ys = [1] * 3; zs = [1]*3
>>> es, ns, us = body_to_enu(yaws, pitches, rolls, xs, ys, zs)
>>> es
array([1., 1., 1.])
>>> ns
array([1., 1., 1.])
>>> us
array([-1., -1., -1.])
```

functions.name: roktools.geodetic.enu_to_body
documentation:
Converts from ENU to XYZ Body reference frame

This accepts scalars or vector of scalars.

Also, the yaw, pitch and roll can be expressed as radians (radians=True)
or degrees (radians=False)

```pycon
>>> np.round(enu_to_body(0, 0, 0, *body_to_enu(0, 0, 0, 0.2, 0.2, 1.5)), decimals=1)
array([0.2, 0.2, 1.5])
```

functions.name: roktools.geodetic.enu_to_ecef_matrix
documentation:
Computes the transformation matrix from ENU to XYZ (ECEF) and returns
the matrix coefficients stored as a a numpy two-dimensional array

This accepts scalars or vector of scalars.

The latitude and longitude can be expressed either in radians (radians=True)
or degrees (radians = True)

functions.name: roktools.geodetic.enu_to_ecef
documentation:
Converts from East North and Up to Cartesian XYZ ECEF

This accepts scalars or vector of scalars.

The latitude and longitude can be expressed either in radians (radians=True)
or degrees (radians = True)

```pycon
>>> enu = (0.5, 0.5, 1.0)
>>> enu_to_ecef(0, 0, *enu)
(np.float64(1.0), np.float64(0.5), np.float64(0.5))
```

```pycon
>>> enu = (0.0, 0.0, 1.0)
>>> enu_to_ecef(0, 0, *enu)
(np.float64(1.0), np.float64(0.0), np.float64(0.0))
```

```pycon
>>> lons = [90, 180, 270]
>>> lats = [0, 0, 0]
>>> es = [-1, -1, +1]
>>> ns = [1, 1, 1]
>>> us = [1, -1, -1]
>>> dxyz = enu_to_ecef(lons, lats, es, ns, us)
>>> round(dxyz[0][0], 8)
np.float64(1.0)
>>> round(dxyz[0][1], 8)
np.float64(1.0)
>>> round(dxyz[0][2], 8)
np.float64(1.0)
>>> round(dxyz[1][0], 8)
np.float64(1.0)
>>> round(dxyz[1][1], 8)
np.float64(1.0)
>>> round(dxyz[1][2], 8)
np.float64(1.0)
>>> round(dxyz[2][0], 8)
np.float64(1.0)
>>> round(dxyz[2][1], 8)
np.float64(1.0)
>>> round(dxyz[2][2], 8)
np.float64(1.0)
```

functions.name: roktools.geodetic.xyz_to_enu
documentation:
Converts from Cartesian XYZ ECEF to East North and Up given a reference
position in Cartesian absolute XYZ ECEF.

```pycon
>>> xyz = [WGS84_A, 0.0, 0.0]   # Reference position
>>> dxyz = (1.0, 0.0, 0.0)      # Deviation relative to reference position
>>> enu = xyz_to_enu(xyz, *dxyz)  # Conversion to ENU at reference position
>>> enu
(np.float64(0.0), np.float64(0.0), np.float64(1.0))
```

```pycon
>>> enu = (0.5, 0.5, 1.0)
>>> dxyz = enu_to_ecef(0, 0, *enu)
>>> dxyz
(np.float64(1.0), np.float64(0.5), np.float64(0.5))
>>> xyz_to_enu(xyz, *dxyz) == enu
True
```

functions.name: roktools.geodetic.ecef_to_enu
documentation:
Converts from Cartesian XYZ ECEF to East North and Up

This accepts scalars or vector of scalars.

The latitude and longitude can be expressed either in radians (radians=True)
or degrees (radians = True)

```pycon
>>> dxyz = (1, 0, 0)
>>> ecef_to_enu(0, 0, *dxyz)
(np.float64(0.0), np.float64(0.0), np.float64(1.0))
```

```pycon
>>> dxyz = (1, 0.5, 0.5)
>>> ecef_to_enu(0, 0, *dxyz)
(np.float64(0.5), np.float64(0.5), np.float64(1.0))
```

```pycon
>>> enu = (0.5, 0.5, 1)
>>> dxyz = enu_to_ecef(0, 0, *enu)
>>> dxyz
(np.float64(1.0), np.float64(0.5), np.float64(0.5))
>>> ecef_to_enu(0, 0, *dxyz) == enu
True
```

functions.name: roktools.geodetic.haversine
documentation:
Calculate the great circle distance between two points
on the earth (specified in decimal degrees) using the Haversine formula

Return units: kilometers

Extracted from <http://stackoverflow.com/questions/4913349>

```pycon
>>> haversine(0, 0, 0, 0)
0.0
```

Example extracted from <https://rosettacode.org/wiki/Haversine_formula>

```pycon
>>> haversine(-86.67, 36.12, -118.40, 33.94, r=6371.8)
2886.8068907353736
```

functions.name: roktools.geodetic.area_triangle_in_sphere
documentation:
Area = pi\*R^2\*E/180

R = radius of sphere
E = spherical excess of triangle, E = A + B + C - 180
A, B, C = angles of spherical triangle in degrees

tan(E/4) = sqrt(tan(s/2)\*tan((s-a)/2)\*tan((s-b)/2)\*tan((s-c)/2))

where

a, b, c = sides of spherical triangle
s = (a + b + c)/2

<http://mathforum.org/library/drmath/view/65316.html>

\# Compute approximate area of India (3287263 km2)
\>>> pWest = (68.752441, 23.483401)
\>>> pEast = (90.153809, 22.87744)
\>>> pNorth = (76.723022, 32.87036)
\>>> pSouth = (77.45636, 8.119053)
\>>> triangle1 = (pWest,pEast,pNorth)
\>>> triangle2 = (pWest,pEast,pSouth)
\>>> area_triangle_in_sphere(triangle1) + area_triangle_in_sphere(triangle2)
3028215.293314756

methods.name: roktools.geodetic.CoordinatesDisplacement.__init__
documentation:
```pycon
>>> c = CoordinatesDisplacement(xyz=XYZ(0.1, 0.1, 0.1))
>>> c.is_valid()
True
```

methods.name: roktools.geodetic.CoordinatesDisplacement.is_valid
documentation:
```pycon
>>> c = CoordinatesDisplacement()
>>> c.is_valid()
False
```

methods.name: roktools.geodetic.CoordinatesDisplacement.get_xyz
documentation:
```pycon
>>> enu = ENU(-0.416, 5.664, -0.081)
>>> reference_position = Coordinates.from_lla(1.52160871, 41.26531742, 127.0)
>>> c = CoordinatesDisplacement(enu=enu)
>>> c.set_reference_position(reference_position)
>>> xyz = c.get_xyz()
>>> '{:.03f} {:.03f} {:.03f}'.format(*xyz)
'-3.784 -0.517 4.204'
```

methods.name: roktools.geodetic.CoordinatesDisplacement.get_enu
documentation:
```pycon
>>> xyz = XYZ(0.1, 0.1, 0.1)
>>> ref_position = Coordinates(4889803.653, 170755.706, 4078049.851)
>>> c = CoordinatesDisplacement(xyz=xyz, ref_position=ref_position)
>>> enu = c.get_enu()
>>> '{:.03f} {:.03f} {:.03f}'.format(*enu)
'0.096 0.010 0.144'
```

methods.name: roktools.geodetic.CoordinatesDisplacement.get_absolute_position
documentation:
```pycon
>>> enu = ENU(0.096,0.010,0.144)
>>> ref_position = Coordinates(4889803.653, 170755.706, 4078049.851)
>>> c = CoordinatesDisplacement(enu=enu, ref_position=ref_position)
>>> position = c.get_absolute_position()
>>> '{:.3f} {:.3f} {:.3f}'.format(*position.xyz())
'4889803.753 170755.806 4078049.951'
>>> c_displacement = CoordinatesDisplacement.from_positions(ref_position, position)
>>> '{:.1f} {:.1f} {:.1f}'.format(*c_displacement.get_xyz())
'0.1 0.1 0.1'
```

methods.name: roktools.geodetic.CoordinatesDisplacement.get_axu
documentation:
Convert to along-track, cross-track and up for a given direction

```pycon
>>> velocity = ENU(1.0, 1.0, 1)
>>> error = ENU(1.0, 1.0, 0)
>>> error = CoordinatesDisplacement(enu=error)
>>> error.get_axu(velocity)
1.414 0.000 0.000
```

```pycon
>>> error = ENU(1.0, 0.0, 0)
>>> error = CoordinatesDisplacement(enu=error)
>>> error.get_axu(velocity)
0.707 -0.707 0.000
```

```pycon
>>> error = ENU(1.0, -1.0, 0)
>>> error = CoordinatesDisplacement(enu=error)
>>> error.get_axu(velocity)
0.000 -1.414 0.000
```

```pycon
>>> error = ENU(-1.0, -1.0, 0)
>>> error = CoordinatesDisplacement(enu=error)
>>> error.get_axu(velocity)
-1.414 0.000 0.000
```

```pycon
>>> error = ENU(0.0, -1.0, 0)
>>> error = CoordinatesDisplacement(enu=error)
>>> error.get_axu(velocity)
-0.707 -0.707 0.000
```

documentation: Position DOP

documentation: Time DOP

methods.name: roktools.geodetic.Dop.vdop
documentation: Vertical DOP

methods.name: roktools.geodetic.Dop.hdop
documentation: Horizontal DOP

Class that implements computation of Dilution of Precision (DOP)

Check that dop from NMEA GPGSV is the same as the one in NMEA GPGSA message
(further investigations need to be conducted if NMEA generated by u-blox NEO 6P,
discrepancies have been found)

\$GPGSA,A,3,10,07,05,02,29,04,08,13,,,,,1.72,1.03,1.38\*0A
\$GPGSV,3,1,11,10,63,137,17,07,61,098,15,05,59,290,20,08,54,157,30\*70
\$GPGSV,3,2,11,02,39,223,19,13,28,070,17,26,23,252,,04,14,186,14\*79
\$GPGSV,3,3,11,29,09,301,24,16,09,020,,36,,,\*76

```pycon
>>> elAz = [ElAz(63,137),
...         ElAz(61, 98),
...         ElAz(59,290),
...         ElAz(39,223),
...         ElAz( 9,301),
...         ElAz(14,186),
...         ElAz(54,157),
...         ElAz(28, 70)]
>>> dop = Dop(elAz)
>>> f'{dop.pdop():.2f}'
'1.72'
>>> f'{dop.vdop():.2f}'
'1.38'
>>> f'{dop.hdop():.2f}'
'1.04'
```

Verification through the example of DOP computation proposed
in Exercise 6-5 (page 228) of:

Misra, P., Enge, P., "Global Positioning System: Signals,
Measurements and Performance", 2nd Edition. 2006

Increasing 30 degrees the azimuth position of 3 sats (+ one in zenith), doubles the vdop value
leaving pdop approximately constant

```pycon
>>> el_az_list = [ElAz( 0,   0),
...               ElAz( 0, 120),
...               ElAz( 0, 240),
...               ElAz(90,   0)]
>>> dop = Dop(el_az_list)
>>> f'{dop.vdop():.2f}'
'1.15'
>>> f'{dop.hdop():.2f}'
'1.15'
```

```pycon
>>> el_az_list = [ElAz(30,   0),
...               ElAz(30, 120),
...               ElAz(30, 240),
...               ElAz(90,  0)]
>>> dop = Dop(el_az_list)
>>> f'{dop.vdop():.2f}'
'2.31'
>>> f'{dop.hdop():.2f}'
'1.33'
```
